The Beauty of Safety

R tidyverse dplyr ggplot2 Random Forest LightGBM

A comprehensive analysis of chemicals in cosmetic products.

As public concern grows around the safety of ingredients in cosmetics, The Beauty of Safety investigates the presence, frequency, and health implications of hazardous chemicals. Using a government-sourced dataset, I analyze trends in toxic-ingredient usage and model the toxicity of cosmetic products to support safer consumer choices.

Dataset

  • Source: Data.gov — Chemicals in Cosmetics, from the California Safe Cosmetics Program (CA Dept. of Public Health).
  • 110K+ product records with chemical names & CAS numbers, product categories, manufacturers, reported chemical counts, reporting/discontinuation dates, and toxicity concerns (cancer, birth-defect, and reproductive risks).

What I built

  • Data cleaning — removed duplicates and high-missingness columns, dropped irrelevant identifiers, standardized date fields and column structure (tidyverse / dplyr).
  • Feature engineering — computed date differences between reporting events and derived toxicity scores from usage frequency and chemical-count-per-product; encoded categoricals for modeling.
  • Visualization — plotted the top-20 most-reported chemicals (e.g. Titanium Dioxide, Silica), annual usage trends, and toxicity-score distributions across years and categories with ggplot2.
  • Modeling — a Random Forest classifier for low/medium/high toxicity and a LightGBM regressor to estimate harmful-chemical counts per product.

Results

  • 97% accuracy classifying product toxicity (low / medium / high) with Random Forest.
  • 80.2% accuracy predicting the number of harmful chemicals per product with LightGBM regression.
  • Titanium Dioxide was the most frequently reported chemical (known cancer links); Cocamide DEA usage dropped sharply after post-2016 regulation.
GitHub Repo
